description.task: Write a Java program that documents the Spring Framework module hierarchy. Create a class that lists Spring's core modules and their purposes, then simulate which modules a web application would use.
instructions: Register exactly these modules: spring-core, spring-beans, spring-context, spring-web, spring-webmvc, spring-data.
In Main, list all modules, then print 'Web App uses: [module1, module2, ...]'.
description.hint: Spring is modular — you only include the parts you need. spring-core and spring-beans form the foundation.
description.expectedOutput:
Spring Modules:
- spring-core: Core utilities and IoC container
- spring-beans: Bean factory and lifecycle management
- spring-context: Application context and events
- spring-web: Web MVC support
- spring-webmvc: REST and web controllers
- spring-data: Repository and ORM integration
Web App uses: [spring-core, spring-context, spring-web, spring-webmvc]

description.task: Simulate the Spring Bean lifecycle by creating a Java class that implements lifecycle hook methods: initialization, usage, and destruction phases.
instructions: init() prints 'Initializing connection to: [connectionUrl]' and sets connected=true.
queryDatabase() prints 'Executing: [sql]' if connected, throws RuntimeException with message 'Bean not initialized!' otherwise.
destroy() prints 'Closing connection to: [connectionUrl]' and sets connected=false.
In Main, create the bean via BeanContainer, run one query, then destroy it.
description.hint: Spring manages bean lifecycle automatically. @PostConstruct runs after injection, @PreDestroy runs before removal from context.
description.expectedOutput:
Initializing connection to: jdbc:mysql://localhost/mydb
Executing: SELECT * FROM users
Closing connection to: jdbc:mysql://localhost/mydb

description.task: Write a Java program that demonstrates two scenarios: building functionality without a framework (manual) vs using framework patterns (structured). Count the lines of boilerplate code in each approach.
description.context: Spring Framework reduced enterprise Java development complexity drastically by providing infrastructure support, letting developers focus on business logic.
instructions: ManualTransactionService.transferMoney() prints: 'BEGIN TRANSACTION', the transfer message, 'COMMIT TRANSACTION'.
FrameworkTransactionService.transferMoney() prints only: 'Transferred [amount] from [from] to [to]' — no transaction management code.
In Main, call both services with (Alice, Bob, 500.0) and label the output.
After both outputs, print 'Manual: 3 lines | Framework: 1 line'.
description.hint: Frameworks provide infrastructure support — transaction management, security, caching — so developers write only business logic.
description.expectedOutput:
--- Without Framework ---
BEGIN TRANSACTION
Transferred 500.0 from Alice to Bob
COMMIT TRANSACTION
--- With Framework ---
Transferred 500.0 from Alice to Bob
Manual: 3 lines | Framework: 1 line
